title: The Generalized Pitman-Stanley Flow Polytope
speaker: Alejandro Morales Borrero
abstract:
In 1999, Pitman and Stanley introduced the polytope bearing their name along with a study of its faces, lattice points, and volume. This polytope is well-studied due to its connections to parking functions, lattice path matroids, generalized permutahedra/polymatroids, and flow polytopes. Its lattice points correspond to plane partitions of skew shape with entries 0 and 1. Pitman and Stanley remarked that their polytope can be generalized so that lattice points correspond to plane partitions of skew shape with entries 0,1,...,m. Since then, this generalization has been untouched. We study this polytope and show that it can also be realized as a flow polytope of a grid graph. In this talk I will discuss characterizations of its vertices and give formulas for the number of vertices and faces as well as old and new formulas for the number of lattice points and volume in terms of rectangular Standard Young Tableaux.
The new formulas come from the volume polynomial formulas of flow polytopes in terms of vector partition functions.
This is joint work with Maura Hegarty, William Dugan, and Annie Raymond.

proof_stages.label: Classical Pitman-Stanley benchmark
proof_stages.summary: The talk sets up the original Pitman-Stanley polytope PS_n(a) by dominance-order inequalities, recalls its generalized-permutahedron/Minkowski-sum nature, and uses it as the base case for later generalizations.

proof_stages.label: Known volume, lattice-point, parking-function and plane-partition formulas
proof_stages.summary: The classical normalized volume and lattice-point formulas are recalled, with Catalan-indexed summation, parking-function interpretations for volumes, and plane-partition encodings of lattice points via partial sums.

proof_stages.label: Skew dominance-bound variant
proof_stages.summary: A lower vector b is added to impose b <= x <= a in dominance order, producing a skew Pitman-Stanley polytope whose lattice points correspond to skew plane partitions and which connects to generalized polymatroids and lattice-path matroids.

proof_stages.label: Matrix-valued generalized Pitman-Stanley polytope
proof_stages.summary: The main generalized object is defined by replacing one vector x with an m by n matrix whose columns form a dominance chain between a and b, so that column partial sums encode successive level interfaces in plane partitions.

proof_stages.label: Generalized non-skew volume formula
proof_stages.summary: For the non-skew generalized polytope the speaker states a volume formula summing over j dominating the constant m-vector, with multinomial and monomial factors multiplied by counts of standard Young tableaux of a rectangle with prescribed last row, and records consequences such as mixed-volume/log-concavity properties and determinant evaluation of the coefficients.

proof_stages.label: Lattice-point and skew generalized formulas
proof_stages.summary: Analogous lattice-point formulas are stated using descent-corrected binomial factors, and the skew volume and lattice-point formulas are described as piecewise polynomial with pieces governed by relative orders of a- and b-data.

proof_stages.label: Flow-polytope and marked-order-polytope proof mechanisms
proof_stages.summary: The proof strategy realizes the generalized Pitman-Stanley polytope as a grid-network flow polytope to access volume polynomials and faces, while skew formulas are obtained more naturally from marked order polytopes of products of two chains and their linear extensions with prescribed positions.
